mnemonics.title: PARA Method Recall
mnemonics.content: Remember the key HttpServletRequest methods with PARE: Parameters (getParameter), Attributes (getAttribute), Request (getMethod, getRequestURI), Authentication (getAuthType, getRemoteUser)

mnemonics.title: Character Encoding Order
mnemonics.content: For POST requests: SET First (setCharacterEncoding), THEN GET (getParameter). If you get before set, the encoding is already determined!

mnemonics.title: Redirect vs Forward
mnemonics.content: R.E.D. - Redirect = Extra Trip (client makes new request); E = Extra request; D = Different URL shown. Forward = Fast (server transfer); No Extra Trip; Same URL.

acronyms.expansion: Graphics Environment Transfer (joke: GET = Get Everything Transmitted)
acronyms.usage: Used to request data from a specified resource. Should not modify server state.
Used to send data to create/update a resource. Can modify server state.
HTTP mapping: POST=Create, GET=Read, PUT=Update, DELETE=Delete
Content types like text/html, application/json, image/png

visualTips.visualization: Think of the servlet as a restaurant: Browser (Customer) → HTTP Request (Order) → Server (Kitchen) → Servlet (Chef) → Process → Response (Food back to Customer)
Draw a timeline: [Client]──param──→[Servlet]──attr──→[Forwarded JSP]. Parameters come from OUTSIDE (client), Attributes are CREATED INSIDE (server)
Draw two paths: Forward = Straight hallway (one request passes through multiple doors). Redirect = Reception desk (client asked to go somewhere else, makes new request)

analogies.analogy: Like a filled-out admission form at a hospital - contains all patient info (parameters), history (headers), vital signs (attributes), and patient ID (session)
Like a reply letter - has the letterhead (status), the content type (letter vs email), and the actual message body
Like the fields on a paper form - the client fills them in, the servlet reads them. Each field has a name and value.
Like sticky notes placed on a document by office workers - internal communication between different parts of the same request processing
Request scope = a phone call (exists only during the conversation). Session scope = a meeting that continues across multiple interactions.
